title: How Smart Contract Costs Are Set
description: Follow the chain from Plutus benchmarks to on-chain cost model parameters — how the price of running a smart contract gets decided.

stops.title: What Are Cost Models?
stops.narrative:
Every time a Plutus smart contract runs on Cardano, the node must know how much to charge for each operation. This is what cost models define: a mapping from each builtin function (addition, division, hash comparison, etc.) to an abstract cost in CPU and memory units called ExUnits.

These abstract costs are consensus-critical — every node in the network must compute the EXACT same ExUnits for the same transaction, regardless of whether it's a Haskell node, a Rust node (Amaru), or anything else. If nodes disagree, the chain forks.

The cost model parameters are stored as protocol parameters, meaning they can only be changed through the [governance process](node:governance-action).

stops.title: Technical Parameters
stops.narrative:
[Cost models](node:cost-models) live in the Technical parameter group alongside poolPledgeInfluence, stakePoolTargetNum, and collateralPercentage.

Changing any Technical parameter requires [CC](node:cc) approval at 2/3 majority and [DRep](node:drep) approval at 67%. This means a cost model update — like the recent +75% repricing of integer division — must go through the full governance process.

The benchmarking is done by the Plutus team, but the decision to apply those benchmarks on-chain is a community governance decision. The Plutus team can't unilaterally change what smart contracts cost.

stops.title: The Governance Path
stops.narrative:
To update [cost models](node:cost-models), someone submits a Protocol Parameter Change [governance action](node:governance-action) specifying the new cost model coefficients. This costs 100,000 ada in refundable [deposit](node:deposit-mechanism).

The action goes through the standard lifecycle: up to 6 epochs of [voting](node:voting), [ratification](node:ratification) at an epoch boundary when thresholds are met, [enactment](node:enactment) at the following epoch boundary.

The [Guardrails Script](node:guardrails-script) checks that the proposed values fall within permitted ranges. This prevents someone from submitting a cost model that prices everything at zero (which would allow unlimited computation per block) or at infinity (which would make smart contracts unusable).

stops.title: Automated Safety Checks
stops.narrative:
The Guardrails Script is the automated enforcement layer. For [parameter changes](node:action-param-change), it validates that proposed values stay within the constitutional bounds defined in Appendix I.

For [cost models](node:cost-models) specifically, this means the benchmark-derived coefficients must be reasonable — not so low that blocks become computationally overloaded, and not so high that smart contracts become prohibitively expensive.

The script runs as part of transaction validation. If a parameter change proposal violates the guardrails, the transaction is rejected before it even reaches the [voting](node:voting) stage.

stops.title: Constitutional Limits
stops.narrative:
The Constitution's Appendix I defines the permitted ranges for all protocol parameters. For [cost models](node:cost-models), this creates a constitutional floor and ceiling on smart contract pricing.

The [CC](node:cc) evaluates cost model changes against these constitutional bounds. Even if the benchmarks say a function should cost X, the CC can vote against the change if it believes the impact would violate constitutional tenets — like Tenet 2 (predictable, reasonable transaction costs) or Tenet 3 (no unreasonable barriers to application development).

This is the human judgment layer on top of the automated [guardrails](node:guardrails-script).

stops.title: When the Price Changes
stops.narrative:
A [cost model](node:cost-models) update gets ratified like any other [parameter change](node:action-param-change): [CC](node:cc) 2/3 + [DRep](node:drep) 67% at an epoch boundary.

Once ratified, the new costs take effect at the NEXT epoch boundary. This gives developers a window to check if their contracts still fit within budget. The recent van Rossem hard fork repricing explicitly warned about 'potentially breaking contracts' — existing scripts using heavy integer division may exceed their ExUnit budgets under the new pricing.

This is the tradeoff: honest pricing protects node operators from subsidizing underpriced computation, but retroactively increases costs for deployed contracts.
